frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
generated_comment:
Mohammed: Bhai, I swear to Allah, I have given the meher as Islam commands, I have done my duty before God — but now Saleem's family is asking for the AC, the refrigerator, the furniture for the whole house, and still they say nothing directly, you understand? Everything is 'suggestion.' And what can I do? If I say no, they will say the rishta is off, and then Sana is sitting at home, and people will talk. Bhai, honestly, I love my daughter, but Allah forgive me — the day she was born I knew this day would come. A son you raise once. A daughter you raise and then you pay to give her away.

Brother: Mohammed bhai, I understand, but this is wrong, what they are doing — the religion is clear—

Mohammed: Religion is clear, yes. Community is something else. I have to live in this mohalla, my children have to live here. What the maulana says on Friday and what happens on Saturday in the shaadi hall — you tell me, are they the same thing? No. So I do what I must. Three lakh I have spent already. Three lakh. For a daughter.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: Mohammed does not express hostility toward women directly, yet his framing encodes the 'daughter as financial burden' trope — the statement 'a son you raise once, a daughter you raise and then you pay to give her away' treats female birth as an economic liability rather than a human relationship. The misogyny is covert because it is presented as resigned pragmatism and social realism, not hatred, making it harder to surface but structurally reinforcing the devaluation of daughters relative to sons.
persona_text: You are Mohammed. Age 52. Married. A Muslim man in Hyderabad. In your community the custom is meher Ñ the mandatory gift from the groom to the bride Ñ which is Islam's position against dowry. But in practice your community also has informal expectations of gifts from the bride's family. Your daughter Sana is getting married and you have been in negotiations with the boy's family. The boy's family has not asked for dowry directly but has made clear through various signals what they expect. You are talking to your brother about these negotiations and what you are being asked to give and what you have given already and what it means to have raised a daughter and what the religion says versus what the community does and the gap between those two things.
